prompt: A Japanese Edo-period woodblock print in the precise style of Katsushika Hokusai, circa 1830. The image depicts a vast mountain rendered not as a natural peak but as a landscape of nested bowls and ridges — a terrain of multiple valleys and slopes, some shallow, some deep, cascading down toward one lowest point in the far distance. The mountain's surface is rendered in Hokusai's characteristic flat indigo, prussian blue, and ochre color fields, divided by bold black outlines, no Western shading or gradient — only flat planes of color separated by line. A lone pilgrim figure — tiny against the mountain, dressed in traditional travelling robes and sandals, carrying a walking staff — stands on a ridge. He does not look at the horizon or the distant valley. He looks only at his feet, at the immediate slope beneath him. He is taking one small step downward, following only what is directly below him. He cannot see the lowest valley from where he stands — a ridge blocks his view. He knows only the direction of the ground beneath his next step. Scattered across the mountain at other ridges and false valleys are the faint ghost-prints of the same pilgrim figure — the same man, at earlier positions, each one a step taken, each one slightly lower than the last, forming a trail of descending footprints that winds through the terrain following the local slope, not the optimal path. One ghost-figure sits stranded in a shallow bowl — a local minimum — unable to descend further, though the true valley is visible far below. The composition follows Hokusai's diagonal structure — the mountain fills the frame dramatically, the pilgrim small and determined in the lower third. Border in the style of a Thirty-Six Views print. Woodblock print texture throughout — visible grain, slight ink variation, the faint registration marks of a hand-pulled print. Prussian blue, indigo, ochre, vermillion, and black only. No text, no labels, no mathematical notation.
